OMICs Analysis - Phylogeny training Chapter 1 Introduction

OMICs Analysis - Phylogeny training OMICs Analysis 2026-07-15 Chapter 1 Introduction These is our main training resource for phylogeny manipulation and graphic representation. We focus on the following subjects NEWICK FORMAT: Content and meaning Usage of the main PACKAGES for phylogeny analysis RECIPES for frequent tasks Specific tasks Newick format mini-manual Phylogenies mini-manual Distances Bootstrapping and support values Root Graph: ggtree (graphs) and its main functions Manipulate phylogeny: treeio Metadata managing: df o treeio-> ggtree Frequent processes in visualization Tip and node labels Metadata addition in tips and nodes Basic functions of ggtree Root and re-root (first: basic concepts) Rotate branches Cut or resize an edge Eliminate branches Cut phylogeny by internal node Parent, child, ancestor and MRCA identification Convert treeio.R* to tree_object_handling.R: phylo, tibble, ggtree, igraph Create: tree_explore_and_transforme.R: ancestor, child, MCRA, siblings, root, cut, prune Keep in mind that phylogenies are created with two main goals: To verify the phylogenetic position of know strains To eliminate or re-label redundant or problematic strains To test different representations using the matetadata To deliver final phylogeny to customers So it is important that phylogenetic distances, support values, as well as relevant data are clearly visible in the phylogeny images :) (Big font, color: high contrast)
Chapter 2 Newick Format 2.1 APPENDIX1 1: Converting to a R phylo object (see apendix below)

Chapter 2 Newick Format Load the neccesary libraries for this section library("ape") #We use this library to read the newick texts library("ggtree") Newick format is a way of representing a phylogeny (a tree) with parentheses and commas. Its basic elements are: Branches Nodes Leaves: Nodes with no descendants, also colled tip points Internal nodes: Nodes with descendants A Minimal newick tree has only branches. There is a branch for each comma and each internal parenthesis pair. # ( , (,) ) # ^ ^ internal parenthesis pair. # ^ ^ external parenthesis Lets obtain this tree in phylo format tree<-"(,(,));" tree<-read.tree(text=tree) And now we can use ggtree to generate an image ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab() Figure 2.1: A minimal newick tree Here you can see tip points and internal nodes colored in red and blue, respectively ggtree(tree)+ geom_tippoint(size=3, color="red")+ geom_nodepoint(size=3, color="blue") The tree leaves (tip points) can be labeled Labels correspond to clades (species, strains) tree<-"(cat,(dog,fox));" tree<-read.tree(text=tree) ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab() You can indicate different lengths for each branch The length reflects the amount of evolutionary change of the branch tree<-"(cat:0.6,(dog:0.1,fox:0.1):0.2);" tree<-read.tree(text=tree) ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab() You can also label internal nodes. Internal nodes are common ancestors for a group of leaves tree<-"(cat:0.6,(dog:0.1,fox:0.1)Anc2:0.2)Anc1;" tree<-read.tree(text=tree) ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab()+geom_nodelab() … or instead of a node label you can use a bootstrap value Bootstrap values indicate the support of a given node. Here, the event with fox and dog having a common ancestor has a confidence of 0.9 tree<-"(cat:0.6,(dog:0.1,fox:0.1)0.9:0.2)0.98;" tree<-read.tree(text=tree) ggtree(tree,)+geom_tiplab()+geom_nodelab() Here we have a more complex example tree<-"(Bovine:0.69395, (Gibbon:0.36079, (Orang:0.33636, (Gorilla:0.17147, (Chimp:0.19268, Human:0.11927)0.5:0.08386 )0.9:0.06124 )0.7:0.15057 )0.8:0.54939,Mouse:1.21460)0.9;" tree<-read.tree(text=tree) ggtree(tree)+geom_tiplab()+geom_nodelab() 2.1 APPENDIX1 1: Converting to a R phylo object (see apendix below) All of the following functions can create a phylo object from a newick file ape::read.tree("example_phylo.nwk") %>% class phyloseq::read_tree("example_phylo.nwk") %>% class phytools::read.newick("example_phylo.nwk") %>% class treeio::read.newick("example_phylo.nwk") %>% class
